claims.claim: Cross-reference identifier 'permissions_pagelevel_xp' is a valid page identifier
claims.evidence: page-permission-management.md front matter line 4: identifier: permissions_pagelevel_xp

claims.claim: Anchor 'Permission requirements for page operations' exists on the hub page
claims.evidence: page-permission-management.md line 175: '## Permission requirements for page operations'

claims.claim: Page permissions control which roles can perform specific operations
claims.evidence: page-permission-management.md line 22: 'Page permissions allow you to grant permissions to roles for website channels, specific content tree sections, and individual pages and folders in the form of an access-control list (ACL).' Six ACL operations confirmed in WebPageAclPermissions.cs (Display, Read, Create, Update, Delete, Synchronize).

claims.claim: 'reading, creating, updating, and deleting pages' are valid page permission operations
claims.evidence: WebPageAclPermissions.cs defines six constants: READ='Read' (line 17), CREATE='Create' (line 23), UPDATE='Update' (line 29), DELETE='Delete' (line 35), DISPLAY='Display' (line 11), SYNCHRONIZE='Synchronize' (line 41). The four operations named in the text (reading→Read, creating→Create, updating→Update, deleting→Delete) are confirmed valid. The use of 'such as' correctly indicates a non-exhaustive list, omitting the more specialized Display and Synchronize operations.

claims.claim: Page permissions apply to 'website channels, specific content tree sections, and individual pages and folders'
claims.evidence: page-permission-management.md line 22: 'Page permissions allow you to grant permissions to roles for website channels, specific content tree sections, and individual pages and folders in the form of an access-control list (ACL).' Exact wording match with hub page.

page-permission-management.md line 22: '...in the form of an access-control list (ACL).' Also confirmed by source code: WebPageAclPermissionEvaluator.cs (entire class is the ACL evaluator), WebPageAclInfo, WebPageAclMappingInfo, WebPageAclRoleInfo — all ACL-prefixed entities.

claims.claim: Cross-reference identifier 'permissions_pagelevel_xp' is valid (Channel manager section)
claims.location: Section: Common system roles, line 103 (page_link tag)
claims.evidence: Same as claim 1. page-permission-management.md front matter line 4: identifier: permissions_pagelevel_xp

claims.claim: Anchor 'Edit page permissions' exists on the hub page
claims.location: Section: Common system roles, line 103 (anchor parameter)

claims.claim: Channel managers can configure page permissions in a website channel
claims.evidence: page-permission-management.md lines 132-148 describe the 'Edit page permissions' workflow. The channel admin bypass (WebPageAclPermissionEvaluator.cs lines 83-87) confirms that channel admins have full access. The hub page line 121-124 documents the 'Channel admin bypass' tip confirming Administrator role and ManagePermissions permission holders bypass ACL checks.

researchRecommendationChecks.notes: Link text 'Page permissions' (Data permissions section) and 'page permissions' (Channel manager section) are descriptive — readers know they'll land on page permission documentation. REC-005 specifies writing link text that makes sense without surrounding text. Both link texts pass this test. No conflict with invariants.

domainInsightChecks.title: Multi-layer parameter architectures require per-layer disambiguation
domainInsightChecks.notes: The Data permissions section properly disambiguates application permissions (preceding paragraph) from data permissions (page permissions and workspaces). The cross-reference anchors ensure readers land on the correct layer-specific section.
